scope: executable async/scheduling/lifecycle/error-channel hard-negative fixture expansion; no product behavior or exact admission changes

after_summary.note: Not every matrix class receives a brand-new assertion in this issue. Classes not directly asserted here are mapped to existing executable fixtures or checked reporting artifacts, and this issue adds a single local suite for the highest-risk cross-language regression boundaries.

covered_boundaries: Promise.then arbitrary receiver versus proven PromiseLike receiver
fulfilled versus rejected Promise channels
throwing callback obligations
Promise.catch recovery while preserving Promise boundary
new Promise multi-settlement ordering
setTimeout versus setImmediate one-shot task scheduling
queueMicrotask versus Promise continuation scheduling
clearTimeout versus cancelAnimationFrame lifecycle
requestAnimationFrame versus microtask scheduling
AbortSignal cancellation/liveness versus Promise rejection
receive value versus receive status
channel send versus assignment
goroutine scheduling versus direct call
defer callback timing versus direct order
select readiness/default versus direct receive
asyncio.gather ordering versus asyncio.wait first-completed behavior
asyncio.sleep timer scheduling versus direct None
async for lifecycle versus ordinary for
asyncio.wait_for timeout versus asyncio.shield cancellation propagation
asyncio.create_task versus ensure_future lifecycle
run_coroutine_threadsafe versus to_thread scheduling
asyncio.run future drive versus returning an undriven coroutine
generator yield lifecycle versus eager materialization
spawn handle lifecycle versus direct await
join all-settled behavior versus select first-ready behavior
block_on future drive versus returning an undriven future
CompletionStage callback timing versus sync direct call
Future.get versus cancellation
Future timeout wait versus status check
CompletionStage handle versus whenComplete
CompletableFuture.allOf versus anyOf
ScheduledExecutor one-shot delay versus interval lifecycle
stream versus parallelStream lifecycle
Task scheduling versus direct await
async let child task lifecycle versus local await
async sequence iteration versus ordinary sequence iteration
try await exception channel versus plain await
throwing versus non-throwing continuation settlement channels
Thread scheduling versus direct method call
Fiber yield/resume lifecycle versus block yield callback demand
raise exception channel versus return success channel
ensure cleanup ordering versus ordinary sequential cleanup

existing_complementary_coverage.covers: literal versus dynamic Promise aggregates, all-fulfilled versus first-settled/all-settled channels, Promise.any all-rejected boundary, and aggregate Promise-boundary preservation
Promise.finally safe settlement passthrough, rejection override, unsafe handler, possible-thenable, and parameterized-callback boundaries
setInterval repeated liveness, clearInterval versus clearTimeout, scheduler.yield, scheduler.wait, requestAnimationFrame, and queueMicrotask/direct-callback boundaries
query-level async/sync unit-boundary rejections for JS/TS, Python, Go channel/concurrency, and broad Promise protocol families
query-level Python async for, async with, and async comprehension lifecycle boundaries
query-level Ruby yield versus return and direct block-call callback boundaries
Rust async closure versus synchronous closure and async block reporting hard negatives
Swift async closure and async-let source-protocol reporting boundaries

local_crates_verification.command: cargo run -q -p nose-cli -- verify crates --max-violations 0 --recall-loss-report target/recall-loss.issue-657.crates.json

notes: This issue intentionally records executable fail-closed behavior instead of opening exact admission.

The new suite uses value-fingerprint hard negatives for runtime/protocol distinctions that the helper observes directly; unit-wrapper async/sync boundaries remain covered at query level because they are represented outside the value-fingerprint payload.

The first narrow run initially exposed that Python and Swift async-function wrappers are not visible to value_fingerprint alone; those assertions were kept out of the new equivalence suite and left to existing query-level fixtures.
